items.myth: You must use os.path for filesystem work.
items.reality: pathlib covers almost all path needs with a cleaner API and is the modern recommendation; os.path is the legacy equivalent.
items.example: Compare: `os.path.join(os.path.dirname(p), 'out.txt')` vs `Path(p).parent / 'out.txt'`. Both work; the second is shorter, type-safe, and chainable.

items.myth: A Path object reads the disk when you create it.
items.reality: Constructing a Path is pure string manipulation; nothing touches the filesystem until you call methods like exists() or read_text().
items.example: `p = Path('does/not/exist.txt')` succeeds instantly — no IO. `p.exists()` returns `False` (now it touches the disk); `p.read_text()` raises `FileNotFoundError` (also touches the disk).

items.myth: os.environ changes persist to your shell after the program ends.
items.reality: Setting os.environ only affects the current process and its children; it does not modify the parent shell's environment.
items.example: Run a script that does `os.environ['FOO'] = 'bar'`. After it exits, in your shell `echo $FOO` is still empty — the shell's env was untouched.
